tips.text: Static injection (no pump) gives longer contact time and is often better for capturing slow binders than flow.
Detecting a larger molecule binding to a smaller one gives a bigger signal. Immobilize the small partner when possible.
This is wavelength-interrogated SPR — the same principle as top-end lab instruments, just without the angular motor.
Seeing a noisy or drifting baseline? Open the Spectroscopy tab to check the transmission spectrum — it shows optical issues instantly.
For biosensing applications, higher immobilization density means more ligand on the surface and a larger binding signal.
Static injection works well for low-concentration samples — the sample stays in contact longer without being washed away.
The gold surface is the sensor — never touch it. Fingerprints and scratches permanently destroy sensitivity.
After inserting a new chip, always calibrate before starting an experiment. Calibration sets the S-pol reference.
Compress your chip before first use to flush air pockets from the microchannels and stabilize the flow path.
With a 1 mL syringe, push slowly and steadily. A jerky injection creates a pressure spike artifact on the sensorgram.
Rinse the inlet ports with buffer before loading a new chip to remove residual salt from the previous experiment.
Noisy baseline after chip loading? Try 2–3 slow buffer flushes before calibrating — it usually clears debris.
Each of the 4 flow cells is optically and fluidically independent. You can run up to 4 different analytes in one run.
For immobilization, give the ligand the full 30 minutes. Ligand density directly determines your binding signal amplitude.
After regeneration, wait for the baseline to fully restabilize before injecting the next analyte concentration.
Water in the flow cell blocks light and shows as extreme signal noise. Always prime with buffer before loading a chip.
Don't over-compress the chip — excess pressure increases variability. Use the minimum pressure needed to seat the chip.
Injected a bubble? If it's trapped, give a short pulse with the syringe — a brief pressure spike usually dislodges it.
When removing the syringe, twist and pull slowly. A straight fast pull creates backflow and disturbs the surface.
At the end of each session, flush the flow cells with water then blow dry with air to prevent salt buildup.
Run the Affinité CLN-KIT maintenance protocol weekly to keep the fluidic cell clean and your baseline stable.
The fluidic cell can develop micro-cracks over time — replace it annually, before a leak damages your polarizer optics.
Replace luer tubing and connectors once a year. High-usage labs should do it sooner — worn tubing is the most common source of unexplained leaks.
LED brightness degrades after ~1 000 hours of use. If signal is dropping across all channels equally, it's the light source, not the chip.
Temperature and pressure changes both cause baseline drift. A reference channel separates real binding from these bulk effects — always use one.
SPR measures refractive index change, not binding directly. Match your sample buffer to your running buffer to avoid a large bulk shift artifact.
Targets below 1 kDa give weak signal. Expect reliable measurements above 1 kDa at high nM concentrations — smaller molecules need surface optimisation.
For weak binders, dissociation starts the moment the wash begins. A fast signal drop during wash is the off-rate, not a failed experiment.
Your results export as a single Excel file with charts — straight from the Edits tab. No manual graph building needed.
The Affilabs.core Excel export loads directly into TraceDrawer, Prism, and Origin for kinetic fitting and figure generation.
Use the Compression Assistant (BETA) to learn correct sensor compression and confirm no leak before starting an experiment.
Pre-wet your syringe tip with buffer before drawing up sample — this eliminates the tiny air bubble that often forms at the tip and disrupts injection.
SimplexSPR's 4 independent channels let you test 4 concentrations at once — a full dose-response in a single injection cycle.
Affinité offers surface chemistries for both small and large molecules. Contact them before choosing your immobilisation strategy — info@affiniteinstruments.com
Affinité can help with assay development: buffer scouting, regeneration conditions, surface chemistry. Reach out before troubleshooting blindly — info@affiniteinstruments.com
Think we can do better? We'd love to hear from you — info@affiniteinstruments.com
Build and save methods offline — no hardware needed. Ask Sparq to set one up for your experiment.
Each LED channel (A-D) illuminates independently. You can run different analytes in each flow cell in one experiment.
After recording, use the Edits tab to align cycles, trim baselines, and export to Excel in one click.
Rinse with buffer for at least 2 minutes before calibrating — a stable baseline gives a cleaner S-pol reference.
The SPR dip shifts to longer wavelengths when analyte binds. A rising sensorgram means binding is happening.
P-polarisation couples to surface plasmons; S-polarisation does not. The P/S ratio cancels LED intensity drift automatically.
Gold chips are single-use. If you see a noisy or flat signal after regeneration, it is time for a new chip.
Use short regeneration pulses (10-15 s) and check the baseline recovery before committing to a full injection cycle.
Inject all 4 channels within 15 seconds of each other — the software handles the timing skew, but wider gaps make alignment harder in Edits.
